We often think and talk about changes in the weather; but how rarely do we consider these things with respect to God, who directs them! We must notice God’s glory not only in thunder and lightning, but also in more common and less dramatic weather changes, such as snow and rain. Nature leads all creatures to seek shelter from a storm; and should people alone be without refuge? Oh that people would listen to the voice of God, who in many ways warns them to flee from coming wrath and invites them to accept his salvation and find happiness. The mistaken view people have about God’s direction is especially clear when they complain about the weather, though the entire year proves the foolishness of their complaints. Believers should avoid this; no days are bad as God makes them, although we make many bad through our sins.

Thoughtful consideration of God's works will help us accept all his providences. Just as God has a powerful, freezing north wind, he also has a thawing, comforting south wind: the Spirit is compared to both because he both convicts and comforts. The best people understand very little about the wonderful qualities of God’s nature and government. Those who, through grace, know much of God realize that what they know is nothing compared to what remains to be known, and what will be known when perfection comes.

Elihu ends his discussion with some important statements about God's glory. Light always exists, but it is not always visible. When clouds appear, the sun is darkened even on a clear day. The light of God’s favor shines toward his faithful servants, though it may not always be seen. Sins are like clouds and often prevent us from seeing the bright light that is in God's face. Also, regarding those thick clouds of sorrow which often darken our minds, the Lord has a wind that passes and clears them away. What is this wind? It is his Holy Spirit. Just as the wind disperses and sweeps away the clouds gathered in the sky, so the Spirit of God cleanses our souls from the clouds and fogs of ignorance and unbelief, of sin and lust. From all these clouds the Holy Spirit frees us in the work of regeneration. And from all the clouds that trouble our consciences, the Holy Spirit sets us free in the work of consolation. Now that God is about to speak, Elihu delivers a few words as the summary of his discussion: With God is terrible majesty. Eventually, all people will fear him.
